h1Title: Impacted Wisdom Teeth: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Wisdom teeth — the third permanent molars — become impacted when insufficient space in the dental arch prevents full eruption. Impaction affects approximately 25% of adults in Western populations and is one of the most common indications for oral surgery worldwide, with roughly 3.5 million extractions performed annually in the United States alone. Partially erupted wisdom teeth are vulnerable to pericoronitis — bacterial infection beneath the overlying gum flap — and to developing dentigerous cysts. Fully impacted teeth can resorb adjacent second-molar roots. Extraction is the definitive treatment; most uncomplicated third-molar removals are day-case procedures under local anaesthesia with rapid recovery.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 25% of adults have at least one impacted third molar; up to 72% of UK adults aged 20-30 show radiographic evidence of one or more impacted wisdom teeth
keyStats.demographics: Most commonly presents in the late teens and twenties; equally distributed between sexes; mandibular impactions outnumber maxillary impactions 2:1
keyStats.avgAge: Radiographic impaction most often identified between ages 17 and 25
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 3.5 million wisdom tooth extractions are performed annually in the US (Basrani 2019); figures are similar in proportion across Europe and Australia

definition: Impacted wisdom teeth (ICD-10: K01.1) describes third molars that fail to erupt fully into their normal functional position in the dental arch due to lack of space, adjacent tooth obstruction, or abnormal tooth angulation. The mandibular third molar is the tooth most frequently impacted (lower wisdom tooth, positioned at the angle of the jaw); the maxillary third molar (upper wisdom tooth) is the second most common. Classification by angulation divides impactions into mesioangular (tipping toward the second molar, approximately 43% of cases), vertical (12%), distoangular (3%), and horizontal (3%). Classification by depth (Pell and Gregory) and by relationship to the ramus assesses surgical difficulty. A partially impacted tooth has broken through the gum but not reached its final position, leaving a tissue flap (operculum) over part of the crown; a fully (completely) impacted tooth is entirely covered by bone and/or gum. Associated pathology includes pericoronitis, caries of the distal surface of the second molar, dentigerous cysts, odontogenic infections, and rarely root resorption of the adjacent second molar. Oral and maxillofacial surgeons and experienced oral surgeons manage this condition; general dentists identify and refer.

typesClassification.description: The wisdom tooth tilts toward the adjacent second molar at an angle of 11-79 degrees. It is the most common type (around 43%) and can impinge directly on the second molar root.
The tooth is upright but blocked from eruption by the ramus or adjacent molar. Represents about 38% of mandibular impactions.
The tooth lies roughly sideways, 90 degrees to normal, pointing directly at the second molar root. Carries the highest risk of second-molar root resorption.
The tooth tilts backward, away from the second molar and into the ramus. Often the most technically difficult to extract because the ramus limits access.
The crown has penetrated bone but remains covered by mucosa; the partial tissue flap creates a pocket (operculum) susceptible to pericoronitis.
The tooth is partially or fully enclosed in the mandibular or maxillary bone, requiring bone removal (ostectomy) during surgical extraction.

primarySymptoms: Persistent dull or throbbing pain at the back of the jaw, worsening on biting, that localises to the retromolar area where the third molar is attempting to erupt.
Acute pain and swelling at the back of the lower gum (pericoronitis) where bacteria colonise the flap of tissue overlying a partially erupted wisdom tooth, often accompanied by a foul taste.
Swollen, red, and tender gum overlying the impacted tooth — the operculum — which bleeds easily on probing and may discharge pus.
Trismus (restricted mouth opening) caused by inflammation spreading to the masseter or pterygoid muscles adjacent to an infected impacted lower third molar.
Referred otalgia (ear pain) or temporomandibular-area discomfort misattributed to ear infection or jaw-joint problems, originating from the proximity of the third molar to the auriculotemporal nerve.
Caries in the distal surface of the second molar caused by the food-trapping gap between the impacted wisdom tooth and the adjacent molar, often inaccessible for cleaning.
Jaw swelling and facial asymmetry when pericoronal infection spreads into the submandibular or buccal space.
Headache and ipsilateral cervical lymphadenopathy as pericoronitis triggers regional immune response.
A painless jaw cyst (dentigerous cyst) developing around the crown of a fully impacted wisdom tooth, sometimes discovered incidentally on a dental panoramic radiograph.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurring mild soreness or pressure at the back of the jaw in late teens, coinciding with third-molar eruption timing, especially when dietary habits narrow the retromolar space.
Partial eruption of a wisdom tooth crown visible at the back of the mouth with a tissue flap over part of it — an operculum — that traps food and is tender to touch.
A pericoronal radiolucency wider than 3 mm around an unerupted third molar on a dental X-ray, indicating early cyst formation or infection.
emergencySigns: Rapidly expanding jaw swelling with fever, trismus, and difficulty swallowing or breathing — signs of spreading odontogenic infection into fascial spaces such as Ludwig angina, requiring emergency hospital admission.
High fever (above 38.5°C), tachycardia, and inability to open the mouth more than 1 cm in a patient with a known infected wisdom tooth — risk of airway compromise demands urgent surgical assessment.
Acute stridor, drooling, or voice change in the context of dental infection — indicates impending airway obstruction and constitutes a surgical emergency.

causes.cause: Insufficient jaw arch length relative to tooth size
causes.description: The jaw did not grow large enough to accommodate four additional molars. Evolutionary reduction in human jaw size over thousands of years, driven by dietary change from tough raw food to cooked soft food, has made dental arch crowding the norm rather than the exception. A 2019 meta-analysis estimated arch-length discrepancy as the primary cause in over 80% of cases.

causes.cause: Mesioangular tilting obstructed by the second molar
causes.description: The most common impaction pattern: the third molar erupts at an angle and the distal cusp of the second molar acts as a mechanical block. The tooth cannot continue along its eruption path and becomes wedged.

In some individuals, particularly those with dense mandibular bone, the third molar eruption force is insufficient to penetrate overlying cortex, resulting in complete bony impaction. This is the most difficult surgical removal scenario.
A third molar developing horizontally or distally cannot follow a normal eruption path regardless of available space.
Third molars forming later than average are at higher risk of being blocked by teeth that have already completed their settlement, leaving less room for eruption.

riskFactors.factor: Narrow dental arch or jaw crowding
riskFactors.description: Arch-length discrepancy is the dominant predictor of impaction; individuals with crowded dentitions are at substantially higher risk of third-molar impaction.
The window of third-molar eruption; monitoring with panoramic radiography is standard at age 17-18 in most dental guidelines.
Twin studies indicate that third-molar impaction has a heritable component; individuals with shorter mandibular arch length or obtuse gonial angles are at greater risk.
A partial tissue covering traps food and bacteria, increasing pericoronitis risk; studies report pericoronitis in 6-10% of partially erupted lower third molars per year.
One episode of pericoronitis carries a 40-50% risk of recurrence within 12 months; recurrence is a strong indication for extraction per NICE guidance.
Difficulty cleaning the retromolar region accelerates plaque accumulation, increasing caries risk on the second molar and infection risk at the impacted site.
Smokers have significantly higher rates of post-extraction dry socket (alveolar osteitis) — up to 12% vs 2-3% in non-smokers — and delayed healing after wisdom-tooth surgery.

affectedDemographics: Peak incidence of clinical presentation between ages 17 and 25 years in both sexes.
Approximately 25% of adults in Western populations have at least one impacted third molar (NICE CG26 background data).
Mandibular third molars are impacted approximately twice as often as maxillary third molars.
East Asian populations may have slightly lower rates of third-molar impaction, associated with shorter average arch-length discrepancy in some cohort studies.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical examination and dental radiography. Clinically, the back of the mouth is examined for partially erupted crowns, opercular tissue, tenderness, swelling, and trismus. A dental panoramic radiograph (OPG/DPT) is the first-line imaging investigation and shows the angulation, depth, and root morphology of all third molars simultaneously, including their relationship to the inferior alveolar canal. Cone-beam CT is reserved for situations where the panoramic radiograph shows the inferior alveolar nerve canal crossing the roots, bifid roots, or other anatomical complexities that raise the risk of nerve injury during extraction — typically identified by darkening of the root, deviation of the canal, or narrowing of the canal on OPG. Periapical radiographs supplement OPG for detailed root assessment of individual teeth. The Winter and Pell-Gregory classification systems, applied to the panoramic radiograph, stratify surgical difficulty and guide the operator (general dentist vs oral surgeon vs maxillofacial surgeon). Infection is evaluated clinically by palpation of regional lymph nodes, assessment of trismus, and temperature measurement; severe cases require panoramic, CT scan, and full blood count with CRP to delineate spread and systemic involvement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line image showing angulation, depth, root number and curvature, nerve-canal relationship, and pericoronal space of all third molars
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient bites on a localiser while the machine rotates around the head in under 20 seconds. Low radiation dose, result available immediately. Reviewed with the patient at chair-side.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detailed view of root morphology and bone around a single third molar; supplements OPG for surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small sensor or film is placed inside the mouth behind the wisdom tooth. The exposure takes under one second. Minimal discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: 3-D mapping of root-to-nerve proximity, cortical perforation, and complex root forms; used to reduce risk of permanent inferior alveolar nerve injury
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient sits or stands in the scanner for a 10-20 second exposure. Higher detail than conventional X-ray; reviewed by the surgeon before operation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures pocket depth distal to the second molar, detecting bone loss caused by the impacted third molar pressing against the adjacent root
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blunt probe is slid between tooth and gum alongside the second molar; measures take under one minute with mild pressure sensation.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and C-reactive protein
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses severity of systemic infection in pericoronitis or spreading fascial space abscess before hospital admission
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venepuncture blood draw; laboratory results typically within 4-6 hours in emergency settings.

treatmentOverview: Management is guided by the presence or absence of symptoms, radiographic pathology, and patient age. NICE guideline CG26 (2000, updated 2020) and the AAOMS evidence-based guidance advise that asymptomatic, fully erupted third molars with no associated pathology do not require prophylactic extraction; surveillance with annual radiographic review is appropriate. When extraction is indicated — recurrent pericoronitis (two or more episodes), unrestorable caries, root resorption of the adjacent second molar, dentigerous cyst, or intractable pain — the procedure is performed as a day-case under local anaesthesia, IV sedation, or general anaesthesia depending on surgical complexity and patient preference. The extraction technique varies by impaction depth and angulation: soft-tissue impactions require a mucoperiosteal flap and suturing; partially bony impactions need bone removal with a surgical bur; fully bony impactions may require tooth sectioning (odontectomy). Mean operating time for a straightforward impacted lower third molar is 20-45 minutes; total visit time is 1-2 hours. Post-operative care includes analgesics (ibuprofen 400 mg and paracetamol 1 g, alternating four-hourly), antibiotics only if evidence of spreading infection (amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 5 days, or metronidazole 400 mg three times daily if penicillin-allergic), and chlorhexidine mouthwash from day 2. The most common complication is alveolar osteitis (dry socket), occurring in 2-3% of routine cases and up to 12% in smokers. Permanent inferior alveolar nerve injury causing lower-lip numbness affects approximately 0.35-0.9% of cases in published audits, with CBCT-guided surgery reducing this substantially.

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical extraction (odontectomy) under local anaesthesia
medicalTreatments.description: Mucoperiosteal flap raised, bone removed to expose the crown, tooth sectioned if needed, socket curetted and sutured. Performed chair-side for most cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Definitive treatment; over 99% of extractions complete without significant intra-operative complication in the hands of trained oral surgeons

medicalTreatments.name: IV sedation-assisted extraction
medicalTreatments.description: Midazolam or propofol titrated IV for anxious patients; the patient is conscious but relaxed and amnesic. Surgeon still uses local anaesthesia. Suitable for complex cases or high dental anxiety.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces peri-operative anxiety to acceptable levels in 90-95% of sedation-eligible patients

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily (5 days) for spreading infection
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for pericoronitis with systemic signs or early fascial space spread. Metronidazole 400 mg three times daily added for anaerobic coverage or as sole agent in penicillin allergy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves localised pericoronitis in 70-80% when combined with irrigation; does not replace surgical extraction

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs and paracetamol for post-operative analgesia
medicalTreatments.description: Ibuprofen 400 mg and paracetamol 1 g alternating every 4 hours provides superior analgesia to opioids for third-molar extraction pain and reduces opioid prescription in most dental surgery contexts.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NRS pain score reduction of 2-3 points vs placebo in randomised trials of third-molar surgery; evidence level Ia (Cochrane 2019)

medicalTreatments.name: Chlorhexidine 0.12-0.2% mouthwash twice daily from day 2
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces bacterial load in the post-extraction socket, decreasing alveolar osteitis incidence. Alcohol-free formulation preferred to avoid mucosal irritation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Chlorhexidine rinses reduce dry-socket incidence by approximately 40% vs placebo (Cochrane systematic review)

surgicalOptions.name: Simple surgical extraction under local anaesthesia
surgicalOptions.description: Flap raised, minimal or no bone removal, tooth removed intact or after sectioning. Used for soft-tissue or shallow bony impactions.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete extraction rate >99%; dry-socket incidence 2-3% in non-smokers

surgicalOptions.name: Complex odontectomy with bone removal and tooth sectioning
surgicalOptions.description: Used for deeply impacted or horizontal teeth; surgical bur removes overlying bone, tooth is sectioned into manageable pieces. Typically performed in a surgical unit by an oral surgeon or maxillofacial surgeon.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful complete extraction in >98% of cases; permanent nerve injury rate 0.35-0.9% at experienced centres

surgicalOptions.name: Coronectomy (intentional partial odontectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Root tips are intentionally left in situ when they are intimately associated with the inferior alveolar canal and removal would risk permanent nerve injury. The crown is removed and the root polished and covered with bone graft or allowed to osseointegrate.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Prevents permanent nerve deficit in high-risk cases; root migration requiring completion extraction in 6-12% over 5 years

alternativeTreatments.name: Pericoronal irrigation with saline or chlorhexidine
alternativeTreatments.description: Used to temporise acute pericoronitis before extraction; a syringe cleans under the operculum to remove food debris and reduce bacterial load. Evidence supports this as bridge therapy only — extraction remains definitive.

alternativeTreatments.name: Operculectomy
alternativeTreatments.description: Surgical removal of the opercular tissue flap over a partially erupted wisdom tooth. Some evidence suggests it reduces pericoronitis recurrence as an alternative to extraction in selected cases with sufficient eruption potential, though long-term data are limited.

whySeeSpecialist: An oral surgeon or oral and maxillofacial surgeon should be consulted when the panoramic radiograph shows the inferior alveolar nerve canal close to or crossing the roots, when systemic infection is present, or when previous attempts at extraction have been abandoned. Complex bony impactions and distoangular impactions are associated with higher complication rates and are best managed by surgeons with high extraction volume.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a dentist experienced in surgical extractions or an oral surgeon for straightforward cases. For deeply impacted teeth with nerve-canal proximity identified on OPG or CBCT, choose an oral and maxillofacial surgeon at a hospital-based unit with high annual extraction volume and access to CBCT imaging.
hospitalCriteria: Access to CBCT imaging for nerve-proximity cases
IV sedation or general anaesthesia facility for complex or anxious patients
Emergency airway management capability if spreading infection is present
Oral pathology laboratory for any associated cyst tissue
keyFacilities: Hospital-based oral and maxillofacial surgery departments
Specialist oral surgery practices with surgical suite and sedation
Dental schools with oral surgery clinics
Community dental services for straightforward impactions
preventionStrategies: Attend dental check-ups at least annually from age 17 with panoramic radiographic assessment of wisdom teeth to identify impaction before symptoms develop.
Maintain meticulous cleaning of the retromolar area with an angled toothbrush or water flosser to reduce pericoronitis risk in partially erupted wisdom teeth.
Avoid smoking; smoking multiplies post-extraction dry socket risk 3-4 times and impairs wound healing.
Follow dental advice regarding elective extraction of high-risk impacted teeth before age 25, when roots are shorter and bone is more pliable, reducing complication rates.
lifestyleModifications: Apply cold packs to the cheek for 20 minutes on and off in the first 24 hours to reduce swelling after extraction.
Eat soft foods (yoghurt, soup, scrambled eggs) for the first week post-operatively; avoid hard, crunchy, or chewy foods.
Avoid rinsing vigorously or using a straw for the first 24 hours to protect the blood clot in the socket and prevent dry socket.
Rinse gently with warm saline from day 2 and use prescribed chlorhexidine mouthwash as directed.
Do not smoke for at least 72 hours after extraction, ideally longer — smoking is the single largest modifiable risk factor for dry socket.
Return to the dentist or surgeon if pain worsens after day 3, the socket develops a foul odour, or facial swelling increases rather than decreases.

recommended: Soft protein foods (eggs, fish, yoghurt, tofu) to support healing from day 1-7 post-extraction
Cold foods and drinks (ice cream, cold water) in the first 24 hours to reduce bleeding and pain
Warm, salt-water rinsing from day 2 to keep the socket clean
Adequate hydration with at least 1.5-2 L of water daily to support systemic recovery
avoid: Hard, crunchy foods (nuts, crisps, raw carrots) for 2 weeks to protect healing socket
Alcohol during antibiotic therapy and for 24 hours post-surgery
Hot liquids in the first 24 hours, which can dissolve the forming clot
Tobacco and smoking for at least 72 hours post-extraction — ideally 2 weeks

exerciseGuidelines: Rest on the day of extraction. Light walking can resume the following day. Strenuous exercise, contact sports, and activities causing significant increase in heart rate should be avoided for at least 3-5 days post-extraction to prevent post-operative bleeding.
dailyManagement: Take analgesics regularly as prescribed for the first 48-72 hours rather than waiting for pain to become severe.
Irrigate the socket gently with a syringe provided by the surgeon from day 5-7 onward if debris accumulates.
Attend post-operative review at 1-2 weeks for suture removal if non-resorbable sutures were placed.
Monitor for dry socket (severe pain starting day 3-4, empty socket, foul taste); attend the dental practice immediately for socket dressing.
Complete the full antibiotic course if prescribed to prevent incomplete treatment of any spreading infection.
prognosis: The prognosis after wisdom tooth extraction is excellent in the majority of patients. Acute pericoronitis resolves after extraction in over 95% of cases. Alveolar osteitis (dry socket) occurs in 2-3% of routine extractions and 10-12% in smokers; it is painful but self-limiting, resolving with socket dressing within one to two weeks. Permanent inferior alveolar nerve damage causing persistent lower-lip numbness affects fewer than 1% of cases in CBCT-guided series and 0.35-0.9% in standard extraction series. Lingual nerve injury causing tongue numbness is rarer still at under 0.1%. Long-term outcomes are defined by the prevention of pathology: patients who retain an asymptomatic fully impacted tooth with no pericoronal cyst, no second-molar root contact, and a crypt that shows no expansion may be surveyed annually rather than subjected to prophylactic extraction; fewer than 12% of such teeth develop pathology after age 25 in longitudinal studies. Overall, the risk-benefit balance favours elective removal before age 25 for symptomatic or radiographically high-risk impactions.
recoveryTimeline: Acute swelling and pain peak at 48-72 hours and typically resolve by day 7-10. Sutures are removed at 7-10 days. Complete socket healing takes 3-6 weeks for soft tissue and 6-12 months for complete bone fill. Most patients return to normal activity within 3-5 days.
complications: Alveolar osteitis (dry socket) — the most common complication, occurring in 2-3% of cases (up to 12% in smokers); presents as worsening pain after day 3 from loss of the protective blood clot; treated with socket dressings.

Inferior alveolar nerve injury causing lower-lip and chin numbness — temporary paraesthesia in 2-8% of cases, permanent in under 1%; risk highest for deeply impacted teeth with nerve-canal contact on CBCT.
Lingual nerve injury causing tongue numbness or taste disturbance — rare (under 0.1%) but occasionally permanent; risk increased with flap retraction in the lingual space.
Secondary infection and post-extraction osteomyelitis — presents with worsening pain, fever, and purulent socket after apparent initial healing; treated with surgical debridement and antibiotics.
Dentigerous cyst formation around a retained unerupted wisdom tooth — reason for annual panoramic surveillance of non-extracted impacted teeth.
Root resorption of the adjacent second molar — reported in 1-4% of cases with mesioangular or horizontal impaction; detected on periodic OPG and is an absolute indication for extraction.

supportResources.name: American Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons — Patient Information
supportResources.description: AAOMS patient pages on third-molar removal, recovery, and surgical risks.

supportResources.name: NHS — Wisdom Teeth Removal
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service patient guide to wisdom tooth extraction, risks, and aftercare.

confusedWithConditions.name: Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ) Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both conditions cause jaw pain and limited mouth opening. TMJ disorder arises from joint and muscle dysfunction; pain is typically pre-auricular, worse on jaw movement, and associated with joint clicking. Impacted wisdom-tooth pain is retromolar, worsens on biting, and is associated with a visible or radiographically confirmed impaction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Odontogenic Infection / Dental Abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pericoronitis from an impacted wisdom tooth involves only the pericoronal soft tissue and is not primarily a pulpal infection. A classic dental abscess arises from a necrotic tooth pulp (often carious), produces a periapical radiolucency at the root apex on X-ray, and can involve any tooth. Both can cause facial swelling and require urgent treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dentigerous Cyst
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An impacted wisdom tooth without a cyst has a normal follicular space of up to 3 mm. A dentigerous cyst is a distinct fluid-filled sac forming when that space exceeds 3-5 mm, exerts progressive bone expansion, and shows a well-defined corticated radiolucent margin on OPG. Clinically, a cyst often presents as a painless jaw swelling rather than acute pain.

relatedConditions.name: Dentigerous Cyst
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common odontogenic cyst arises around the crown of an impacted third molar; all impacted wisdom teeth require periodic panoramic surveillance to exclude cyst formation.

relatedConditions.name: Odontogenic Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Pericoronitis from an impacted wisdom tooth can progress to a deep-space odontogenic infection such as Ludwig angina if not treated promptly.

relatedConditions.name: Ludwig Angina
relatedConditions.relevance: The most dangerous complication of an infected impacted lower wisdom tooth; bilateral submandibular and sublingual cellulitis can threaten the airway and requires emergency hospitalisation.

faqs.question: Do impacted wisdom teeth always need to be removed?
faqs.answer: No. Asymptomatic, fully impacted wisdom teeth with no associated cyst, no second-molar root contact, and a stable pericoronal space may be monitored with annual radiographs rather than extracted. NICE and AAOMS guidelines recommend extraction only when pathology is present or high risk is established.

faqs.question: What does an impacted wisdom tooth feel like?
faqs.answer: An impacted wisdom tooth typically produces dull, throbbing pain at the back of the jaw that worsens when biting. Partial eruption creates a swollen, tender gum flap at the rear of the mouth. Some fully impacted teeth cause no symptoms until a cyst or adjacent tooth damage is found on X-ray.

faqs.question: How long does wisdom tooth extraction recovery take?
faqs.answer: Swelling and pain peak at 48-72 hours and resolve within 7-10 days for most patients. Soft-tissue healing completes in 3-6 weeks; full bone fill takes 6-12 months. Most people return to normal diet and activity within 3-5 days.

faqs.question: What is pericoronitis?
faqs.answer: Pericoronitis is a bacterial infection beneath the gum flap covering a partially erupted wisdom tooth. It causes acute pain, swelling, bad taste, and restricted mouth opening. Mild cases are treated with irrigation and antibiotics; recurrent episodes are an indication for extraction.

faqs.question: What is a dry socket after wisdom tooth removal?
faqs.answer: Dry socket (alveolar osteitis) occurs when the blood clot protecting the empty socket dissolves prematurely, exposing bare bone. It causes severe pain starting around day 3-4 after extraction, often with a foul taste. Treatment is a medicated socket dressing applied by the dentist; the socket heals in 1-2 weeks.

faqs.question: Can an impacted wisdom tooth damage adjacent teeth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mesioangular and horizontal impactions can press directly against the second molar, causing root resorption in 1-4% of affected cases. An associated dentigerous cyst can also erode adjacent bone and tooth roots. This is one reason high-risk impactions are removed electively.

faqs.question: Is wisdom tooth extraction performed under general anaesthesia?
faqs.answer: Most wisdom tooth extractions are done under local anaesthesia in a dental chair. IV sedation is offered for anxious patients or complex cases. General anaesthesia in a hospital is reserved for surgical difficulty, medical comorbidities, or patients unable to cooperate with conscious treatment.

faqs.question: How do dentists classify impacted wisdom teeth?
faqs.answer: Surgeons use the Winter classification (angulation: mesioangular, vertical, horizontal, distoangular) and the Pell-Gregory classification (depth and ramus relationship) to assess surgical difficulty from dental panoramic X-rays, guiding referral and anaesthetic planning.

faqs.question: What is the risk of nerve damage from wisdom tooth removal?
faqs.answer: Permanent inferior alveolar nerve injury causing lower-lip numbness occurs in approximately 0.35-0.9% of extractions at experienced centres. CBCT scanning identifies high-risk root-nerve relationships before surgery, allowing coronectomy to be chosen where nerve contact is confirmed.

faqs.question: Can I eat normally after wisdom tooth extraction?
faqs.answer: Soft foods only for the first week: yoghurt, soup, scrambled eggs, mashed vegetables. Hard, crunchy, and chewy foods should be avoided for 2 weeks to protect the healing socket. Most patients return to a normal diet by 2-3 weeks.

faqs.question: Why do wisdom teeth become impacted?
faqs.answer: The human jaw has evolved to be smaller over millennia of dietary change, leaving insufficient space for the four third molars to erupt fully. Around 25% of adults in Western populations have at least one impacted wisdom tooth as a result of this arch-length discrepancy.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for an infected wisdom tooth?
faqs.answer: Amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 5 days is first-line for pericoronitis with systemic signs. Metronidazole 400 mg three times daily is added for spreading infection or used alone in penicillin allergy. Antibiotics reduce spread but do not replace surgical extraction.

faqs.question: What is coronectomy?
faqs.answer: Coronectomy is intentional partial removal of a wisdom tooth where the crown is extracted but the roots are left in place because they lie against the inferior alveolar nerve. It prevents permanent nerve damage while eliminating the symptomatic crown. About 6-12% of retained roots eventually migrate and require completion extraction.

faqs.question: At what age should wisdom teeth be assessed?
faqs.answer: Dental guidelines recommend a panoramic radiograph at age 17-18 to assess third-molar development and impaction risk. Elective extraction before age 25 carries lower surgical risk because roots are shorter and bone is more pliable.

faqs.question: Can impacted wisdom teeth cause headaches or ear pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. The proximity of the lower third molar to the auriculotemporal nerve can produce referred pain in the ear and temple mimicking ear infection or TMJ disorder. Once the wisdom tooth infection or impaction is treated, the referred pain resolves.

symptomKeywords: bad taste at back of mouth
metaTitle: Impacted Wisdom Teeth: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Impacted wisdom teeth affect 25% of adults, causing jaw pain, infection, and cysts. Symptoms, surgical extraction, and recovery reviewed by specialists.
